mappings.rationaleAI: Engineering directly addresses the nature and scope of engineering as a discipline (B), including its professional education and functions (B.1) and all branches named in the outline — civil, mechanical, electrical, chemical, aerospace, and nuclear engineering (B.2).

Agriculture covers the nature and scope of agricultural practice including crop production, livestock, genetic engineering, and agricultural economics (C), and its subsystems map directly to the subdivisions of agricultural sciences — soil science, plant production, and animal production — named in C.2.

Mechanical engineering is explicitly named as one of the branches of engineering in B.2, and the article covers core competencies in mechanics, thermodynamics, materials science, CAD/CAM, and emerging fields including mechatronics and biomechanics.

Robotics is an interdisciplinary field spanning mechanical and electrical engineering (B.2) that also exemplifies bionics (D.1, D.1.a) through the mimicry of biological locomotion, neural control systems, and manipulation in robotic design.

AI exemplifies D.1.b (use of the human brain as a model for computers and information-processing devices) through neural networks and deep learning, and its system-level optimization and decision theory dimensions connect to systems engineering and operations research (D.2).

The computer article covers electrical/electronics engineering as a branch (B.2) and directly exemplifies D.1.b — the human brain as a paradigm for computer memory and information-processing devices, from early mechanical calculators to modern AI-enabled systems.

Computer science is the theoretical and applied discipline underlying electrical/electronics engineering (B.2) and its algorithms, computability theory, and AI subfields bear directly on computation as a model of information processing per D.1.b.

Electronics is explicitly named as a branch of engineering in B.2 (electrical and electronics engineering), and the article covers transistors, integrated circuits, semiconductor devices, and signal processing that underpin all modern electronics engineering.

Soil science is the first subdivision of agricultural sciences explicitly listed in C.2; this article covers edaphology (soil-organism relations), pedology (formation and morphology), soil classification, and soil threats such as acidification and desertification.

Animal production is explicitly listed as a subdivision of agricultural sciences in C.2; animal husbandry covers livestock management including feeding, breeding, health management, and production systems for cattle, sheep, pigs, poultry, and aquaculture species.

History of agriculture is the direct content of C.1 (history of the agricultural sciences), a coverage-priority path; the article spans Neolithic origins through the Green Revolution, covering all major agricultural transitions from multiple independent domestication centers globally.

Maize is the world's highest-volume grain crop and a foundational subject of plant production science within the agricultural sciences (C.2), covering its domestication, genetics, breeding, pest management, and industrial uses including ethanol production.

History of technology directly addresses A (history of the technological sciences), tracing tool-making and engineering from the Stone Age through the Industrial Revolutions to 20th-century computing and biotechnology, providing the historical foundation for the entire section.

Telecommunications falls within electrical and electronics engineering (B.2), covering signal transmission, modulation, network architecture, and the progression from telegraph through telephone, radio, and internet that defines the communications engineering branch.

Edison exemplifies the history of the technological sciences (A) through his industrial research paradigm and inventions in electrical power distribution, light, and communications; his work spans what is now electrical engineering (B.2).

Tesla's development of AC power systems places him in the history of the technological sciences (A), and his polyphase AC motor and induction motor designs are foundational contributions to electrical engineering (B.2).

Ford's moving assembly line and Fordist mass production system are landmarks in the history of technological sciences and industrial engineering (A), and his automotive manufacturing work connects to mechanical engineering as a branch (B.2).

The machine article covers the history of technological devices from simple machines through industrial machinery (A), and its treatment of mechanical advantage, thermodynamic systems, and power transmission is foundational to mechanical engineering (B.2).

Manufacturing covers the transformation of raw materials through engineering processes, tracing the history of the technological sciences from prehistoric handicraft through the Industrial Revolutions (A) and spanning multiple engineering branches (B).

Semiconductors are the material foundation of all modern electrical and electronics engineering (B.2); the article covers p-n junctions, transistors, integrated circuits, and the quantum physics underlying semiconductor device operation.

Faraday's discovery of electromagnetic induction laid the historical foundation for electrical engineering (B.2) and is a landmark in the history of the technological sciences (A), leading directly to the electric motor and practical electrical power generation.

Food production and processing is the ultimate output of agricultural sciences (C.2), and the article covers industrial agriculture, food systems, and food security topics within the scope of plant and animal production subdivisions named in C.2.

Biochemistry is explicitly noted in its summary as applying to agriculture through soil chemistry and pest control (C.2); its bridging of biological and chemical engineering through biotechnology also exemplifies the interscience disciplines in D.

Shen Kuo's Dream Pool Essays documented foundational technological innovations — compass, movable-type printing, drydock engineering, and canal locks — making him a significant figure in the history of the technological sciences (A) from a non-Western perspective.

The tool article traces human technological development from prehistoric stone tools through metalworking and mechanization (A), providing the earliest layer of the history of technological sciences and its connection to human evolution and manufacturing.

Paper's invention in Han dynasty China and its subsequent global spread represent a significant episode in the history of technological sciences (A), connecting cellulose chemistry, printing technology, and information dissemination in the history of applied material processes.

Silicon is the dominant material of the semiconductor industry underpinning electrical and electronics engineering (B.2); the article covers its use in transistors, integrated circuits, and solar cells and the materials science that enables modern electronics.

Electric batteries are a core electrical engineering technology (B.2), covering electrochemical energy storage from primary cells through lithium-ion and grid-scale systems that underpin portable electronics, electric vehicles, and renewable energy infrastructure.

Leonardo's notebooks contain systematic engineering investigations of mechanical devices, hydraulics, and optics that preceded modern technological science, making him a notable figure in the history of the technological sciences (A) as a Renaissance precursor.

Textiles represent one of the oldest industries and were central to the First Industrial Revolution; the article's coverage of fibre, yarn, and fabric production processes across history makes it relevant to the history of the technological sciences (A).

Radar is an electrical and electronics engineering technology (B.2) using radio wave propagation, signal processing, and phased-array electronics across applications in air traffic control, meteorology, and autonomous vehicles.
